questions.question: Order the main sections of a research proposal as they appear, narrowing from problem to plan.
questions.explanation: A proposal moves from Motivation (why we need this study) to Background (the state of the art), then to the Research Question and Hypothesis, and finally the Methods that can answer it. This mirrors the narrowing from a broad problem to a concrete plan.

questions.question: Order the three source-misuse errors from LEAST common to MOST common.
items: Lumping citations at the end of a paragraph
correct_order: Lumping citations at the end of a paragraph
questions.explanation: Lumping citations at the paragraph's end is the least common error, claims with no citation happen constantly (mid-frequency), and listing instead of synthesising is the most common and hardest to fix.

questions.question: Order these steps for supporting a real-world motivation, from weakest hook to strongest evidence.
items: Back it with statistics or peer-reviewed future-work sections
Open with an anecdote as a hook
Add news coverage of the problem
correct_order: Open with an anecdote as a hook
Add news coverage of the problem
Back it with statistics or peer-reviewed future-work sections
questions.explanation: An anecdote is only a hook and not enough on its own; news coverage strengthens the case; statistics and the future-work sections of peer-reviewed papers give the strongest support for real-world relevance.

questions.question: Order the steps of building and using a synthesis matrix for one paragraph.
items: Read each row horizontally to find synthesis across sources
Put sources across the top and main ideas down the side
Fill the matching boxes with notes, leaving empties blank
Drop or split rows that are too empty or too full
correct_order: Put sources across the top and main ideas down the side
Fill the matching boxes with notes, leaving empties blank
Read each row horizontally to find synthesis across sources
Drop or split rows that are too empty or too full
questions.explanation: First lay out sources across the top and ideas down the side, then fill the matching boxes (many stay empty). Read horizontally for synthesis, then act on the diagnostics: drop a too-empty row or split a too-full one.

questions.question: Order what each proposal section must convince the reader of, following the section order.
questions.explanation: Following the proposal order, Motivation must convince 'we need this study', Background must show 'you've done your homework', and Methods must convince that the design is correct and replicable.
